title: More Risk, More Information: How Passive Ownership Can Improve Informational Efficiency
abstract: We identify a novel economic mechanism through which passive ownershippositivelyaffects informational efficiency in the cross-section of firms. Passive investors’ inelastic demand lowers a firm’s cost-of-capital, inducing it to take more risk. The higher cash flow variance, in turn, incentivizes active investors to acquire more precise private information, pushing up price informativeness for firms with high passive ownership. High passive ownership also implies higher stock prices and higher stock-return variances. An increase in the aggregate size of passive investors amplifies these cross-sectional differences. We also document complementarities in firms’ real investment and investors’ information choices that can cause information crashes.

author.Adrian Buss*: Frankfurt School of Finance & Management,Germanyand CEPR

Passive investors, such as exchange-traded funds (ETFs) or index funds, are nowadays major shareholders of almost all publicly traded companies. In the United States they hold, on average, more than 17% of the shares outstanding, though with large cross-sectional variation across firms.1Not surprisingly, this development has spurred on an intense debate about the impact of passive investing on financial markets, in particular on the informativeness of stock prices and, hence, capital allocation efficiency.

The conventional view is that passive ownership negatively affects the informational content of stock prices because passive investors lack incentives to gather firm-specific information. Yet, empirically, price informativeness tends to be higher for firms with large shares of passive owners. For example,Bai, Philippon, and Savov (2016),Farboodi et al. (2022), andDávila and Parlatore (2022)document that firms with high shares of institutional investors, as well as large firms, have more informative stock prices.Antoniou et al. (2023)even find that a rise in passive ownership increases price informativeness and, as a result, that firms more heavily rely on their stock prices to guide their investment policies. Hence, at a high level, it seems that the implications of passive ownership are more intricate than the conventional view might suggest.

This paper’s objective is to provide new theoretical insights into the relationship between passive ownership and the informativeness of stock prices, as well as firms’ real investment decisions. We identify a novel economic mechanism through which passive ownershippositivelyaffects informational efficiency in the cross-section of firms. The effect is driven by the interaction between firms’ ownership structure, their corporate policies, and investors’ portfolio and information choices. In a nutshell, passive owners’ inelastic demand lowers a firm’s cost-of-capital, inducing it to take more risk. The increase in riskiness, in turn, incentivizes active investors to acquire more private information, and, hence, price informativeness goes up.

The paper is based on a noisy rational expectations equilibrium (REE) model in which investors’ portfolio and information choices, stock prices, and firms’ real investment policies are jointly determined in equilibrium. Indeed, we extend traditional multiasset REE models of joint portfolio and information choice along three key dimensions. First, we model both active and passive investors’ endogenous portfolio choices. Second, we allow firms to differ in their ownership structure. While “index firms” have a nonzero share of passive owners, “nonindex firms” are exclusively owned by active investors. Third, we allow firms to endogenously set their real investment policies and to take into account their ownership structure when doing so. Otherwise, the model is kept as simple and standard as possible to convey the economic mechanisms in the clearest possible way.

Primarily, we use our model to study the implications ofcross-sectional variations in passive ownership. Our main findings can be summarized as follows. First, index firms (or, equivalently, firms with a larger share of passive owners) invest more aggressively in growth opportunities, due to the lower elasticity of passive investors’ demand. Specifically, because the demand of passive owners is driven by the risk-return profile of theindex, changes in the fundamentals of an individual stock will only have a small impact on their demand, thereby rendering the aggregate demand for index firms less elastic (than that of nonindex firms). This, in turn, implies a smaller “variance discount” and, hence, lower marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities and, thus, higher real investments for index firms.

Second, firms with larger shares of passive owners have more informative stock prices. Specifically, the higher cash flow variance of index firms strengthens active investors’ incentives to acquire more precise private information. As a result, the average private signal precision and, in turn, price informativeness are higher for index firms.

Third, index firms have higher stock prices than nonindex firms. In particular, the lower marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities allow index firms to benefit more from the opportunities’ positive (average) return, thereby pushing up their stock prices. Finally, the higher cash flow variance translates into a higher return variance for index stocks.

The economic mechanism strengthens as the number of firms in the index rises. Indeed, the larger the number of firms in the index, the smaller is the impact of each individual firm on the index, and, hence, the lower is the elasticity of passive investors’ demand. Thus, as the number of index firms goes up, the difference between index and nonindex firms’ real investments, informational efficiency, stock prices, and return variances becomes more pronounced.2

Passive investors’ index trading also gives rise to a nonzero correlation among index stocks’ prices and returns, despite their fundamentals and noise shocks being independent. In particular, a change in the price of any index stock or in passive investors’ beliefs about any index stock affects their demand forallindex stocks, thereby creating an endogenous correlation among index firms.

We also discuss how an increase in the aggregate assets under management of passive funds affects the equilibrium. Foremost, it implies an amplification of the cross-sectional differences in real investment, in informational efficiency, and in stock prices. Because of complementarities in firms’ real investment choices and investors’ information choices, it can, however, also lead to an investment and information crash, particularly for nonindex firms.

Finally, we confirm the robustness of our main mechanism and results. To do so, we first show that allowing for heterogeneity in passive ownership (through differences in index weights) leads to similar conclusions. That is, as the weight of a firm in the index goes up, it invests more aggressively in growth opportunities and its stock price is more informative. Second, we demonstrate that allowing for (unlearnable) systematic risk leaves the main mechanism unchanged; though, quantitatively, the effects are slightly less pronounced. Third, we show that our findings carry over to a setting with benchmarked, instead of passive, investors: as the weight of a firm in the benchmark goes up, the firm invests more in growth opportunities, and its stock price becomes more informative (see theInternet Appendix).

Overall, by allowing firms to account for their ownership structure when making corporate decisions, our model generates a rich set of novel and unique predictions about the impact of passive ownership on price informativeness, stock prices, and returns. Many of these predictions are consistent with existing evidence. Moreover, our own empirical analysis lends support to our economic mechanism: firms with larger shares of passive owners tend to have higher earnings variability and more informative stock prices.

Methodologically, we propose a tractable corporate finance addition to an otherwise standard noisy REE framework that allows for “supply-side” adjustments by firms in response to different ownership structures. Indeed, firm characteristics, namely, the mean and variance of firms’ cash flows, are endogenous in our model, a factor that can, as demonstrated, give rise to new and surprising economic forces. Yet, the model remains tractable and yields closed-form solutions.

The paper contributes to three strands of the theoretical literature.3First, it connects to the literature on the information choices of institutional investors. Closest to our work is the paper byBond and García (2021). The authors also study a noisy REE framework with passive and active investors as well as multiple risky assets plus, in an extension, endogenous real investment policies. Moreover, both papers highlight the impact of passive investors’ correlated trading (though this is not essential to our mechanism). Yet, the key mechanisms and the main predictions are markedly different. Specifically, the mechanism inBond and García (2021)is driven by the interaction between investors’ participation choices and their risk-sharing benefits (both absent in our work). In contrast, our economic mechanism arises from the interaction between investors’ endogenous information choices, firms’ risk-taking, and investors’ demand elasticities (all absent in their paper). Indeed, one of our main predictions is ahigherprice informativeness for index stocks whereasBond and García (2021)find the opposite. Furthermore, our results point towardunconditionallyhigher risk-taking by index firms (regardless of fundamentals) whereasBond and García (2021)stress a higher sensitivity of real investment to business conditions (because of managers’ learning from prices).

Also closely related is the work byGarleânu and Pedersen (2018), who study the impact of variations in the search cost for fund managers—in a model with active and direct (passive) investors—and find that asset prices become more efficient as costs decline. Note, however, that direct investors in their model differ from our passive investors along one key dimension: they are unconstrained in their portfolio choice, whereas our passive investors can only invest in the index. Moreover, their single-asset setting does not allow for cross-sectional variations among firms.

Breugem and Buss (2019),Kacperczyk, Nosal, and Sundaresan (2022), andHuang, Qiu, and Yang (2020)also study the impact of institutional investors on price informativeness. Specifically,Breugem and Buss (2019)show that benchmarking reduces the number of shares investors hold for speculative reasons and, therefore, lowers their incentives to collect information. Related,Chen, Bo, and Wang (2022)discuss how different learning technologies shape the link between benchmarking and market efficiency. InKacperczyk, Nosal, and Sundaresan (2022), an increase in passive investing reduces the relative size of active investors and their market power such that active investors shift their attention toward fewer stocks, an action that lowers aggregate price informativeness. Finally, inHuang, Qiu, and Yang (2020), institutional investors are (endogenously) more risk averse and, hence, trade less aggressively on information, thereby harming information aggregation. Indeed, a common theme of these papers is an emphasis on the adverse consequences of institutionalization. In contrast, our paper highlights that passive ownership positively correlates with price informativeness in the cross-section—if one allows firms to take into account their ownership structure in financial markets when making their real investment choices.

Second, our paper relates to the literature studying the impact of institutional investors on financial markets withexogenous(symmetric) information.Cuoco and Kaniel (2011)andBasak and Pavlova (2013)explicitly model institutional investors’ relative performance concerns and document that their portfolio choices push up the prices of firms that are part of a benchmark.Buffa, Vayanos, and Woolley (2022)study a setting with endogenous asset management contracts and document the impact of institutional investors on asset prices.Buffa and Hodor (2023)highlight how benchmarking can create spillovers across assets. While these papers focus on institutional investors in general,Chabakauri and Rytchkov (2020)andBaker, Chapman, and Gallmeyer (2022)explicitly analyze indexing.Chabakauri and Rytchkov (2020)carefully document two opposing forces of indexing—a “lockstep trading effect” and a risk-sharing effect—and study how these forces affect asset returns and welfare.Baker, Chapman, and Gallmeyer (2022)show how a decrease in the fee for passive investing can negatively affect the overall efficiency of the economy because of a corresponding decline in the number of activists (who, in their model, play an essential monitoring role through the use of “voice”). In contrast to these papers, we allow for endogenous information and real investment choices. Doing so delivers many new insights into the impact of institutional investors, driven by the interaction between investors’ and firms’ choices. Our work is also related to that ofKashyap et al. (2021), who show that firms within an index accept riskier projects than do firms outside the index. While a similar result arises in our framework, we focus on the implications for information collection and aggregation.

Third, our paper complements the literature on “feedback effects” that studies how financial markets affect firms’ real investment decisions through firm managerslearningfrom market prices.4For example,Goldstein and Yang (2017,2019) discuss the impact of information disclosures on market efficiency.Chen, Goldstein, and Jiang (2007),Foucault and Frésard (2012,2014),Edmans, Goldstein, and Jiang (2015), andDessaint et al. (2018)discuss managers’ learning in models with feedback effects and provide empirical support for this channel.Goldstein, Schneemeier, and Yang (2020)discuss a fundamental “mismatch” between managers’ and traders’ optimal choice of private information and highlight its impact on price and real efficiency. Importantly, while we are also interested in how financial markets affect firms’ decisions, our economic mechanism is markedly different in that it operates through firms’ ownership structure, not the information environment. Indeed, to distinguish our mechanism from feedback effects, managers cannot learn from stock prices in our model.
This section introduces our economic framework, which extends traditional multiasset REE models ofjointportfolio and information choices along two dimensions: (1) by explicitly accounting for the presence of passive investors and (2) by allowing for endogenous real investment policies.

1.1 Model

Timing

We consider a static model, which we break up into four subperiods, as illustrated inFigure 1. In period 1, thereal investment period, firms decide how much capital to allocate to growth opportunities. In period 2, theinformation choice period, active investors choose the precision of their private signals about firms’ cash flows. In period 3, theportfolio choice period, investors set up their portfolios. While active investors are unconstrained in their portfolio choices, passive investors can invest in an index only. Prices are set such that markets clear. In period 4, theconsumption period, payoffs are realized, and investors consume the proceeds of their investments.

Investment opportunities

Multiple financial securities are traded competitively in financial markets. First, a riskless asset (the “bond”) which has a gross payoff ofrunits of the consumption good and is available in perfectly elastic supply. It also serves as the numéraire. Without loss of generality, we normalize its price to one.

Second, there existNrisky assets (the “stocks”). Each stock,n∈{1,…,N}⁠, is modeled as a claim to a random payoff,fn, which is observable in period 4 only. The supply of each stock,x¯n⁠, is finite (normalized to 1) and its time-3 price is denoted bypn. We define theN×1vectors of payoffs, supplies, and prices as𝟙f≡[f1,…,fN]′,x¯≡[1,…,1]′=1⁠, andp≡[p1,…,pN]′⁠, respectively.

Third, there exists a passive investment option (the “index”) comprising the first2≤M<Nstocks, with index weights based on firms’ market capitalization.5Accordingly, we refer to the firstMstocks as index firms and to the remainingN–Mstocks as nonindex firms. We define the payoff and price of the index asfI≡w′fandpI≡w′p⁠, wherew=[1,…,1,0,…,0]′denotes the vector of index holdings (with the firstMentries equal to the respective stocks’ supplies and the remainingN–Mentries equal to zero).

Investors

There is a continuum of atomistic investors with mass one that we separate into two groups:(1)a fractionΓP>0of passive investors,P⁠, and(2)a fractionΓA=1−ΓP>0of active investors,A⁠.6Each investor,j∈{P,A}⁠, is endowed with initial wealth,W0,j⁠, which we normalize (without loss of generality) to 1.

All investors have constant absolute risk aversion (CARA) preferences over terminal wealthWj:ρρu(Wj)=−(1/ρ)exp(−ρWj)⁠, withρdenoting absolute risk aversion. Investors differ in their investment mandate, as well as in their access to private information.Passive investors,j∈P⁠, can only invest in the bond and the index, and have no access to private information.Active investors,j∈A⁠, can freely invest in all financial securities and can acquire private information about the stocks’ payoffs.

There also exist noise (liquidity) traders with random, not explicitly modeled, stock demands. This assumption, which is standard in the literature, prevents prices from fully revealing the information acquired by active investors and, thus, preserves their incentives to acquire private information in the first place. Noise traders’ stock demands,σx≡[x1,…,xN]′∼N(0,σxIN)⁠, are normally and independently distributed across stocks (withINdenoting anN×Nidentity matrix).7

There exist N firms, each of which is linked to one of the N stocks traded in financial markets. Firms are endowed with assets in place (whose value we normalize to zero) and have access to risky growth opportunities. Investors (and firms) are endowed with unbiased priors. To clearly separate the impact of the ownership structure (our story) from managers’ learning from stock prices (the “feedback” channel), we assume that firms do not learn from prices when making their real investment decisions.

In periodt= 2, active investors can acquire private information about firms’ cash flows. For example, they may study financial statements, gather information about consumers’ taste, hire outside financial advisers, or subscribe to proprietary databases. 2.3 Real investment choices

A new, key feature of our framework is that we allow for endogenous real investment policies. article.3 The Cross-Sectional Implications of Pa:
In this section, we study the impact ofcross-sectionalvariations in passive ownership on firms’ real investment policies, informational efficiency, and stock prices. To do so, we primarily exploit the heterogeneity in passive ownership between index firms (nonzero passive ownership) and nonindex firms (zero passive ownership), while keeping the share of passive investors,ΓP⁠, fixed.

3.1 Real investment

First, we analyze how passive ownership affects firms’ real investment policies.

Theorem 4. For any economy, there exists an M such that for all M ≥ M _ the marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities are always lower for index firms than for nonindex firms. Accordingly, firms that are part of the index invest more in growth opportunities.

To understand the economic intuition behind this result, recall first that the marginal cost of investing in risky growth opportunities is predominantly determined by the higher price discount commanded by investors in response to an increase in cash flow variance. Specifically, as prior variance and, accordingly, posterior variance, go up, the aggregate demand for a stock declines. Thus, for markets to clear, its price must also decline. Importantly, the more sensitive aggregate demand is to changes in variance, the larger the price discount must be and, thus, the higher the marginal cost.

For nonindex stocks, the aggregate demand is solely determined by the demand of the active investors, which is highly sensitive to changes in variance. In contrast, the aggregate demand for index stocks is a combination of active and passive investors’ demand, with the latter being largely variance insensitive. Specifically, consider first—for illustration purposes only—the limit in which the index contains an infinite number of firms. In that case, a change in the cash flow variance of a single index stock leaves the variance of the index—and, hence, passive investors’ demand—completely unchanged. Consequently, the aggregate demand for index stocks is considerably less elastic (than that of a nonindex stock) and, thus, their prices must react less to changes in variance in order to clear the market. Accordingly, the price discount and the marginal costs are lower.Figure 2illustrates the resultant difference in marginal costs between index and nonindex firms.13

The same logic applies for a finite number of index firms. That is, the demand of passive investors reacts less to changes in a stock’s cash flow variance because their demand is driven by the (posterior) variance of theindex, not by that of the stock. Indeed, as the number of index stocks goes up, a change in one stock’s cash flow variance has an increasingly smaller impact on the index variance and, hence, on passive investors’ demand, as well as on aggregate demand. Accordingly, the gap in marginal costs between index and nonindex firms widens as the number of firms in the index,M, increases (see panel A ofFigure 3).

Given that the marginal benefits of investing in growth opportunities are the same for all firms, the lower marginal costs imply a larger investment in growth opportunities for index firms (i.e., for firms with more passive owners).Figure 2illustrates this, with the intersection of marginal costs and benefits representing the optimal real investment. Similarly, as the number of index firms goes up and the gap in marginal costs widens, the difference in firms’ investments in growth opportunities becomes more pronounced. Finally, the larger capital allocation to risky growth opportunities for index firms naturally translates into a higher expected cash flow and a higher cash flow variance.

3.2 Informational efficiency

We now turn to the impact of passive ownership on the informativeness of stock prices.

Theorem 5. Assume M ≥ M _ ⁠ . The average precision of active investors’ private information and price informativeness are higher for index firms than for nonindex firms.

Notably, price informativenessincreasesin the proportion of shares held by passive investors. To understand the economic intuition behind this result, recall that firms with a high share of passive owners (i.e., index stocks) invest more aggressively in risky growth opportunities and, hence, have a higher cash flow variance,σn. The higher cash flow variance lowers the precision of public information and, hence, increases the marginal benefits of private information (cf. Theorem 2).Figure 4illustrates this.15

3.3 Asset prices

Passive ownership also affects equilibrium stock prices and returns.

The higher stock price for firms with higher passive ownership (i.e., index stocks) can be traced back to two effects. First, keeping real investmentξnfixed, the additional demand from passive investors leads to a higher aggregate demand and, hence, pushes up index stocks’ prices. This effect is reminiscent of the “benchmark inclusion subsidy” discussed inKashyap et al. (2021)and present even without endogenous information choice. Second, because firms that are part of the index have lower marginal costs, they can better benefit from the positive (average) return offered by the growth opportunities.16

Not surprisingly, passive ownership also affects stock return moments.

Economic forces similar to those behind the stock-price effects shape the difference in index and nonindex stocks’ expected excess returns. 17 First, keeping real investment fixed, passive investors’ “excess demand” lowers the risk premium for index stocks. Second, index firms’ higher investment in growth opportunities implies a higher posterior variance, σ ^ n ⁠ , which, in turn, leads to a higher excess return. For most of the cases we explored, the second (positive) effect dominates. Panel A of Figure 6 illustrates this. Index stocks usually also have a higher return variance. A combination of two economic forces explains this. First, the higher investment in risky growth opportunities by index firms increases their cash flow variance and, hence, their return variance. Second, keeping real investment fixed, passive investors’ connected trades lower the sensitivity of a stock’s price to its own shocks (pushing down return variance) but, at the same time, increase its sensitivity to the shocks of the other index stocks (pushing up return variance). Overall, the positive effects usually dominate. Accordingly, as the number of stocks in the index goes up and, hence, the real investment channel strengthens, the gap in index and nonindex stocks’ return variances widens. Passive investors’ connected trades also introduce a nonzero return covariance among index stocks, whereas covariances are zero for nonindex stocks and between index and nonindex stocks. Panel C illustrates this. As the number of stocks in the index goes up and, hence, shocks are distributed across a larger number of index stocks, the return covariances decline.

3.4 Extension: Variations in index weights

Our findings are robust to allowing for larger degrees of heterogeneity in the share of passive ownership (than the binary choice between index and nonindex firms). Indeed, we now assume a generic (index) weight vector,w=[w1,…,wN]′⁠.18

Theorem 8. For any economy, there exists a M such that for all M ≥ M _ ⁠ , the marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities shift down as the share of passive ownership increases. Hence, firms with higher passive ownership invest more in growth opportunities. Accordingly, as the fraction of shares held by passive investors increases, so do the average precision of active investors’ private information and price informativeness. Finally, the expected stock price is increasing in the fraction of shares held by passive investors.

The economic intuition underlying Theorem 8 closely follows the arguments discussed above. As the share of passive owners goes up (i.e., the weightwnincreases), passive investors’ less-elastic demand makes up a larger fraction of a stock’s aggregate demand. This lowers the price discount associated with a higher cash flow variance and, hence, leads to a decline in the marginal cost of investing in growth opportunities (for all levels of real investment,ξn).Figure 7illustrates this.19

article.4 Variations in the Share of Passive Inv:
We now discuss the implications of variations in theaggregateassets under management of passive investment funds. To do so, we vary the share of passive investors in the economy,ΓP⁠, and study its impact on firms’ real investment policies, informational efficiency, and stock prices.20

4.1 Amplification

Intuitively, an increase in the share of passive investors strengthens the mechanism discussed in the preceding section and leads to an amplification of the cross-sectional differences between index and nonindex firms. Indeed, one can show that the gap between firms’ marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities generally widens as the share of passive investors increases:

Theorem 9. Assume M ≥ M _ and Γ P < 0.5 ⁠ . An increase in the share of passive investors, Γ P ⁠ , leads to an increase in the difference of the marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities between index and nonindex firms.

All else equal, an increase in the share of passive investors,ΓP⁠, implies that each active investor has to bear more risk. At the same time, posterior precision declines—a consequence of a deterioration in information aggregation. While the former effect implies a higher price discount per unit of risk, the latter implies a higher level of risk. Thus, both effects lead to a higher overall price discount and, hence, unambiguously push up the marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities for index and nonindex firms.

For firms with passive owners, however, two economic forces mitigate this increase in marginal costs. First, because of the lower elasticity of aggregate demand (i.e.,ααI<1⁠), both the higher discount per unit of risk and the higher posterior variance have a smaller impact on an index firm’s value. Second, the increase in the share of passive investors, in general, further lowers the elasticity of aggregate demand (i.e.,αIdeclines further).21While the first effect limits the increase in marginal costs for index firms (compared to nonindex firms), the latter even pushes down the marginal costs for index firms. Consequently, the gap between index and nonindex firms’ marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities widens as the share of passive investors increases. Panel A ofFigure 9illustrates this.

The widening dispersion in firms’ marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities has a variety of immediate implications. First, it translates into more pronounced differences in firms’ real investment policies (panel B). Accordingly, the gap in price informativeness and in stock prices between index and nonindex firms also widens as the share of passive investors goes up (panels C and D, respectively).

4.2 Investment and information crashes

We conclude this section with a brief discussion of how changes in the share of passive investors can cause discontinuities in real investment and information acquisition. Specifically, panels A and B ofFigure 10illustrate an increase in the share of passive investors,ΓP⁠, can lead to investment and information crashes, in particular for nonindex firms.

Fig. 10 Investment and information crashes The figure illustrates investment and information crashes. Depicted are real investment (panel A) and price informativeness (panel B) of index and nonindex firms, as a function of the share of passive investors Γ P ⁠ . Panel C plots the marginal costs and benefits of investing in risky growth opportunities of a nonindex firm, as a function of real investment ξ n for two different levels of the share of passive investors. The graphs are based on the following parameter values: r = 1, ρ = 2, σ μ σ π σ x = 0.3 2 , μ z = 1.3 , σ z = 0.35 2 , π = 0.001 ⁠ , c = 0.4, N = 50, and M = 5. Open in new tab Download slide

The mechanism responsible for these jumps is driven by complementarities in firms’ real investment choices and investors’ information choices. That is, as a firm invests more in risky growth opportunities, its cash flow variance increases which, in turn, strengthens active investors’ incentives to acquire private information. Consequently, a firm’s real investment and active investors’ (average) private signal precision simultaneously increase. If the latter effect is strong enough, it causes—by mitigating the increase in investors’posteriorvariance—a decline in marginal costs. Accordingly, marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities follow a hump-shaped pattern, as illustrated in panel C ofFigure 10.

This hump-shaped pattern in marginal costs, in turn, facilitates the occurrence of investment and information crashes. Specifically, for low shares of passive investors, a “high-investment, high-information equilibrium” materializes (as represented by the black dot in panel C) because of the aforementioned decline in marginal costs. However, as the share of passive investors increases and, thus, marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities shift upward, marginal benefits and costs might “cross” before marginal costs start to decline.22Consequently, a “low-investment, low-information” equilibrium arises (as represented by the black square in panel C). Indeed, an (infinitesimal) small change in the share of passive investors,ΓP⁠, can trigger such an abrupt change in a firm’s investment policy and investors’ information acquisition, explaining the investment and information crashes illustrated in panels A and B ofFigure 10.23

While the preceding discussion illustrates the occurrence of information and liquidity crashes for a specific set of parameter values, one can show that such jumps are, indeed, a broad feature of our framework. Specifically, the following proposition holds:

Theorem 10. For any economy, there exists a combination of μ z and c such that there is, in equilibrium, a discontinuity in real investment (and, hence, in information choice) with respect to changes in the share of passive investors Γ P ⁠ .

Intuitively, a discontinuity arises in equilibrium if the derivatives of marginal costs and marginal benefits with respect to real investment are the same. In that case, a small change in the share of passive investors has a large impact on the equilibrium outcomes. This can only occur in the downward-sloping part of the marginal cost function, as the derivative of marginal benefits is always negative. But, notably, it is always possible to find parameter values forμzandcsuch that the discontinuity arises.24Finally, note that jumps are only possible with an endogenous information choice; that is, with exogenous signal precision, there are no discontinuities.

The same phenomenon might arise for index firms, though (all else equal) for higher levels of the share of passive investors,ΓP(as index firms’ marginal costs are lower).
article.5 Empirical Implications:
Our model delivers a variety of novel predictions, for example, for the informativeness of stock prices, stock-return moments, and corporate policies. In the following, we summarize the key predictions of our model, discuss existing and new evidence that lends support to these predictions, and outline avenues for future research.

5.1 Price informativeness

One of our central cross-sectional predictions is that, perhaps counterintuitively, firms with high passive ownership should have more informative stock prices. This prediction is unique to our model and can be traced back to the interaction between firms’ corporate policies, their ownership structure, and investors’ information choices.

Using the data set prepared by Kacperczyk, Sundaresan, and Wang (2020) , we test this prediction empirically. Specifically, following Bai, Philippon, and Savov (2016) , we run the following panel regression: 25 E n , t + h / A n , t = a + b 1 log ( M n , t A n , t ) + b 2 log ( M n , t A n , t ) × ACTIV E n , t + b 3 log ( M n , t A n , t ) × PASSIV E n , t + e n , t , (16) where E n , t + h / A n , t denotes firm n ’s earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) in year t + h scaled by year t ’s total assets; M n , t / A n , t denotes firm n ’s market capitalization (i.e., stock price times the number of shares outstanding) in year t scaled by year- t total assets; ACTIV E n , t denotes the fraction of stock n held by active investors in year t ; and PASSIV E n , t denotes the fraction of stock n held by passive investors (defined as index funds, ETFs, and quasi-indexers) in year t .

Intuitively, the regression measures how closely current stock prices track future earnings and, hence, how much fundamental information is capitalized in stock prices. In particular, a positive coefficientb3indicates that stock prices correlate more closely with future earnings for firms with large passive ownership and, hence, that their prices are more informative.

Columns 1 and 2 ofTable 1present the results of the regression analysis. Specifically, column 1 shows that the interaction termM/A×PASSIVEis highly statistically significant for a forecasting horizon of 3 years and, hence, that firms with higher shares of passive owners have more informative stock prices.26,27The results for a horizon of 1 year are consistent with this and of the same magnitude (cf. column 2).

Yet, consistent with our results, bothGlosten, Nallareddy, and Zou (2020)andAntoniou et al. Interestingly,Farboodi et al. (2022)show that largegrowthfirms have particularly informative stock prices, a result that endogenously arises in our model because firms with high passive ownership have more informative prices and invest more in growth opportunities. Moreover, consistent with our findings that an increase in the share of passive investors should lead to more pronounced differences in price informativeness, bothBai, Philippon, and Savov (2016)andFarboodi et al. (2022)report that the gap in informativeness has widened over the past years.

5.2 Corporate policies

Key to our economic mechanism is the prediction that firms with high shares of passive owners invest more in risky growth opportunities and, hence, have a higher cash flow volatility.

Consistent with this,Harford, Kecskés, and Mansi (2018)report that a larger share of passive ownership leads to more R&D investment. Similarly,Bena et al. (2017)document, using data for firms in 30 countries, that R&D investments increases significantly after index inclusion. Hence, both papers provide evidence regarding enhanced risk taking of firms with larger shares of passive owners, as predicted by our framework.

Our own analysis provides further evidence that is consistent with these findings and our predictions. Specifically, to test whether higher levels of passive ownership lead to increased levels of earnings volatility, we run the following panel regression: EVO L n , t + h = a + b 1 log ( M n , t A n , t ) + b 2 ACTIV E n , t + b 3 PASSIV E n , t + e n , t , (17) where EVO L n , t + h denotes the h -year future earnings volatility of firm n at time t (and the other variables are defined as before). Intuitively, a positive coefficient b 3 implies higher (future) earnings volatility for firms with larger shares of passive owners.

Column 3 ofTable 1presents the results of the regression analysis and confirms that, consistent with the mechanism of our model, passive ownership is associated with significantly higher levels of future earnings volatility.

5.3 Stock prices and returns

Our model also generates a rich set of cross-sectional asset pricing predictions. First, our model implies an index effect, the magnitude of which positively correlates with passive ownership. In particular, our model predicts that firms that are added to an index and, hence, see their share of passive owners increase, should have persistently higher stock prices. Second, our model predicts more volatile and higher average stock returns for firms with higher shares of passive owners.

An extensive literature, starting with the work byHarris and Gurel (1986)andShleifer (1986), lends support to the prediction of an index effect. Moreover, recently,Pavlova and Sikorskaya (2023)have documented that the size of the index effect is positively related to benchmark intensity, that is, how prominently a stock features in benchmark indexes, exactly as predicted by our model.

Furthermore, exploiting exogenous changes in index membership,Ben-David, Franzoni, and Moussawi (2018)document that stocks with higher passive ownership display significantly higher return volatility and earn a significantly higher risk premium (of up to 56 basis points per month).
article.6 Conclusion:
The growth of passive investing has raised many concerns among market participants and policy makers about its impact on market efficiency, stock prices, and corporate policies.

In this paper, we study the impact of passive ownership on real investment policies, price informativeness, and stock prices. We develop a multiasset noisy REE framework of joint portfolio and information choice in which both active and passive investors endogenously choose their portfolios; firms differ in their degree of passive ownership; and firms take into account their ownership structure when making real investment decisions.

We primarily use our model to study the impact of passive ownership in the cross-section of firms. Our main findings can be summarized as follows. First, firms with a larger share of passive owners invest more aggressively in risky growth opportunities. Specifically, the inelastic demand of passive investors lowers their cost-of-capital and, hence, encourages firms to take on more risk. Second, passive ownership positively affects informational efficiency. That is, the higher cash flow variance (resulting from elevated risk-taking) induces active investors to devote more resources to information acquisition, thereby increasing the informational content of stock prices. Third, firms with high passive ownership have higher stock prices and more volatile stock returns.

An increase in aggregate passive ownership (i.e., the share of passive investors) amplifies these effects and leads to a stronger dispersion in real investment policies, stock prices, and price informativeness across firms. It also leads to a deterioration in informational efficiency and, occasionally, to investment and information crashes.

Similar to how the feedback literature has stressed the impact of learning from stock prices on corporate decisions, our work highlights the impact of firms’ ownership structure on these decisions. Indeed, on the one hand, extensions of our theoretical framework could help to shed light on how passive ownership affects (and interacts with) the compensation schemes of fund managers, optimal index design, or managers’ learning. On the other hand, empirically, more work is needed to establish a causal link between passive ownership and corporate decisions, price informativeness, and stock returns (for recent work on these themes, see, among others,Coles, Heath, and Ringgenberg 2022;Sammon 2022;Sharma 2022;Antoniou et al. 2023;Pavlova and Sikorskaya 2023).

However, the model becomes considerably less tractable. In contrast, an entropy constraint usually favors specialization (or, with CARA preferences, leads to indeterminacy); that is, active investors will learn about a single stock only, contrary to the evidence on fund managers’ information choices.

12 Technically speaking, the impact of passive investors’ connected trades materializes at two points in the price coefficients B and C . First, in the common inverted matrix. In the presence of passive investors, this matrix has negative off-diagonal elements, reflecting the fact that a price increase for one index stock will also increase the price of the index and, thus, lower passive investors’ demand for all index stocks. Second, the effect materializes in the common component of each price coefficient’s second term. In the presence of passive investors, this matrix has positive off-diagonal elements, reflecting the fact that more optimistic beliefs for one index stock raises investors’ beliefs about the index and, thus, will increase passive investors’ demand for all index stocks.

B Systematic Risk

The model presented in the main part of the paper features only idiosyncratic (productivity) shocks. In the following, we present an extension of our base-case framework that also features systematic risk and briefly discuss its implications. The framework differs from the one presented in Section 1 only along a single dimension: the demand of noise traders is given by x = x ˇ + y ⁠ , where σ x ˇ ≡ [ x ˇ 1 , … , x ˇ N ] ′ ∼ N ( 0 , σ x I N ) denotes idiosyncratic noise shocks and σ y ∼ N ( 0 , σ y ) denotes an aggregate noise shock. Thus, the model now features a systematic “discount-rate shock.” The equilibrium in the trading round remains largely unchanged. That is, stock prices continue to be linear functions of fundamentals and noise shocks: p r = A + B f + C x ⁠ . The main difference is that the (co)variance matrix of noise shocks, Σ x , is now nondiagonal, specifically given by σ 𝟙 𝟙 σ Σ x = σ x I N + 1 1 ′ σ y ⁠ . Accordingly, the precision of public stock prices is given by ρ η η Σ p − 1 = ( Γ A ) 2 ρ 2 ( Σ η − 1 ) ′ Σ x − 1 Σ η − 1 .

Moreover, while the functional form of coefficients A and B remains as in Theorem 1, coefficient C becomes ρ η ρ C = ( Γ A Σ ^ − 1 + Γ P w w ′ 1 σ ˜ I ) − 1 ( ( ( 1 − Γ P ) I N + Γ P 1 σ ˜ I w w ′ Σ ˜ ) Γ A ρ Σ η − 1 Σ x − 1 + ρ I N ) ​ .

Investors’ objective in the information round also remains unchanged; specifically, each active investor’s optimization problem is given byEquation (A8). Note, however, that there are now cross-learning effects. That is, the (average) private-signal precision of each stock now also affects price informativeness and, hence, the posterior precision of all other (index and nonindex) stocks. Intuitively, more precise information for one stock allows an investor to better learn about the aggregate noise shock,y, which, in turn, helps her to better learn about the fundamentals of all stocks. This also implies that the active investors’ posterior (co)variance matrix,Σ^⁠, is now nondiagonal, which renders the information-choice problem considerably less tractable. Accordingly, we rely on a numerical algorithm to solve for the equilibrium. Finally, the functional form of the (unconditional) expected stock price,E[pr]⁠, remains as inEquation (A12), with Σxaffecting firm values throughηΣη,Σ˜,Σ^⁠, andσ˜I⁠.Figures A1andA2present the equilibrium with systematic risk for the parameter values used in the main part of the paper. We have confirmed that the same patterns arise across a wide range of parameter choices. First, our main mechanism is robust to the introduction of systematic risk. That is, the marginal costs of investing in growth opportunities continue to be lower for index firms than for nonindex firms (seeFigure A1). As a result, for any level of systematic risk,σy, index firms invest more in risky growth opportunities, which, in turn, incentivizes active investors to acquire more precise private information, and, hence, the prices of index firms are more informative (see panels A to C ofFigure A2). Second, in the presence of systematic risk, the differences between index and nonindex firms are less pronounced (compared to the case of idiosyncratic risk only, that is,σσy=0⁠). Intuitively, with unlearnable systematic shocks, part of the additional risk that an index firm might want to take on cannot be “cushioned” by information acquisition. This, in turn, weakens a firm’s risk-taking incentives. Accordingly, as the amount of systematic risk increases, the difference in price informativeness decreases slightly before it levels off.33Panels A to C ofFigure A2illustrate this effect for variations in the variance of the aggregate noise shock,σy.34Panel D illustrates a similar effect arising in the case of an increase in the number of index firms,M. Specifically, while in the absence of systematic risk an increase in the number of index stocks unambiguously increases the difference between index and nonindex firms’ real investments, the difference declines in the presence of systematic risk.
